skill.name: creating-agents
skill.description: Guides users through creating Claude Code subagents with focused roles, tool permissions, models, storage locations, and reusable templates.
skill.summary: Create focused Claude Code subagents with clear scopes, tool permissions, and templates.
skill.author: BrendanShields

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many high-risk patterns, but review shows most are Markdown code fences, YAML examples, model names, and security-review vocabulary rather than executable code. The confirmed risk is contextual: the skill guides creation of persistent Claude Code agents that may receive Bash, file-write, network-capable tools, or bypassed permission prompts if users choose unsafe settings.

medium_findings.title: Powerful Agent Tool Grants Require Caution
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends selecting minimal tools, but its examples and reference material include Bash plus Write and Edit permissions. This is legitimate agent-authoring guidance, yet unsafe selections could create agents able to run commands or change files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly list Bash, Write, and Edit as configurable tools. The content is instructional rather than malicious, so the risk depends on user-selected permissions.

medium_findings.title: Persistent User-Level Agent Files Are Documented
medium_findings.description: The skill documents project and user agent directories, including a hidden home-directory path. This is expected for Claude Code agents, but user-level agents may affect future projects if created too broadly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths are real storage guidance and include a hidden user directory. The guidance is transparent and not stealthy, which keeps the issue at medium severity.

medium_findings.title: Permission Bypass Mode Is Listed In Reference Material
medium_findings.description: The reference lists bypassPermissions as an available permission mode that skips prompts. This can be useful in controlled workflows but can also remove an important safety boundary for generated agents.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permission mode and its behavior are stated directly in the reference. There is no instruction to prefer it, but documenting it creates a clear safety consideration.

low_findings.title: Backtick Execution Alerts Are Markdown Fence False Positives
low_findings.description: The Ruby and shell backtick findings correspond to Markdown code fences and inline formatting in documentation. I did not find executable Ruby, shell scripts, or runtime command construction in the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are visibly Markdown examples, not code that runs. This explains the large number of external command alerts from static analysis.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts do not correspond to cryptographic functions or hashing code. The reviewed lines contain descriptions, YAML examples, model names, or security checklist text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No cryptographic API, algorithm selection, or secret handling appears at these locations. The alerts are consistent with keyword matching against documentation.

low_findings.title: Network Reconnaissance Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The network-related alerts appear in lists of available tools or review checklist language. I did not find code that performs network scanning, opens sockets, or exfiltrates data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is descriptive markdown, not executable network behavior. The skill does not contain scripts or URLs that would support a stronger finding.

dangerous_patterns.title: Configurable Bash And File-Write Agent Permissions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill teaches how to configure agents with Bash, Write, and Edit. These permissions are not inherently malicious but should be granted only when the agent scope requires them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is confirmed in documentation and templates. The danger is misuse of broad permissions, not hidden execution by the skill itself.

dangerous_patterns.title: Permission Prompt Bypass Option
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference documents bypassPermissions, which skips prompts. Users should avoid this mode unless they fully trust the agent definition and execution context.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is explicitly documented. It is a legitimate option, but it weakens human review of tool actions.

content.value_statement: Building useful subagents is hard when roles, tools, and triggers are vague. This skill provides a structured workflow and templates for focused Claude Code agent definitions.
actual_capabilities: Guides users through defining a subagent purpose, triggers, storage location, tools, model, and system prompt.
Explains Claude Code agent frontmatter fields such as name, description, tools, model, permissionMode, and skills.
Provides templates for reviewer, researcher, and specialist agents.
Shows how to choose project-level or user-level agent storage.
Encourages least-privilege tool selection for custom agents.
limitations: Does not validate generated agent files automatically.
Does not enforce safe tool permissions after an agent is created.
Assumes the user already has Claude Code agent support available.
Templates are generic and require project-specific customization.

use_cases.title: Create a Code Review Agent
use_cases.description: Build a read-only reviewer that checks quality, security, reliability, and project standards.

use_cases.title: Create a Research Agent
use_cases.description: Define a codebase researcher that searches files, explains architecture, and avoids file modification.

use_cases.title: Create a Domain Specialist
use_cases.description: Draft an agent for database, API, testing, or other focused technical work.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Research Agent
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a read-only agent.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a Claude Code research agent for this project. It should read files, search patterns, and summarize findings without editing files.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Code Reviewer
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a code-reviewer agent for our repository. Include trigger phrases, read-only tools, severity categories, and concise output rules.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Specialist Agent
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced setup for a technical domain.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a database specialist agent. Define its scope, allowed tools, model choice, common tasks, and boundaries for migrations.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an Agent Definition
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review before publishing or sharing.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this proposed Claude Code agent definition for unclear triggers, excessive tools, permission risks, and missing constraints.

output_examples.input: Create a read-only agent that explains how authentication works.
output_examples.output: A project-level research agent plan with a clear name, trigger description, Read, Glob, and Grep tools, and a system prompt focused on explanation.

output_examples.input: Create an API specialist agent for endpoint design and integration work.
output_examples.output: A specialist agent outline with API responsibilities, suitable tools, model guidance, output standards, and warnings about broad write permissions.

output_examples.input: Check whether my custom reviewer agent is too broad.
output_examples.output: A permission and scope review that identifies vague triggers, unnecessary Bash access, and changes needed before team use.

best_practices: Start with read-only tools and add write or Bash permissions only when required.
Use precise trigger phrases so Claude selects the agent for the right tasks.
Prefer project-level agents for team workflows and review user-level agents regularly.
anti_patterns: Creating one general agent with all tools for unrelated tasks.
Using bypassed permissions for agents that can edit files or run commands.
Writing vague descriptions that make automatic agent selection unreliable.

faq.question: What does this skill help me create?
faq.answer: It helps create Claude Code subagents with focused roles, tool permissions, model choices, and system prompts.

faq.question: Can it create agents for Codex?
faq.answer: The guidance is written for Claude Code subagents, but Codex users can adapt the role and permission-planning ideas.

faq.question: Does it run commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. The files are markdown guidance and templates. They do not contain executable scripts.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill discusses agents that may receive Bash, file-write tools, or bypassed permission prompts if users configure them unsafely.

faq.question: Should I use user-level or project-level agents?
faq.answer: Use project-level agents for team-specific behavior. Use user-level agents only for trusted personal workflows across projects.

faq.question: What should I customize first?
faq.answer: Customize the description, trigger phrases, allowed tools, and boundaries before adding detailed task instructions.
